2024 · ICLR 2025 · Agents

Navigating the Digital World as Humans Do: Universal Visual Grounding for GUI Agents

Let a GUI agent see only what a person sees — pixels — and act the way a person acts — clicking a coordinate. The missing piece is grounding: turning a plan's words into the exact pixel to click. UGround is a 7B model trained on 10M synthetic web elements that does exactly that, and it lets a screenshot-only agent match or beat agents that read the HTML.

Boyu Gou, Ruohan Wang, Boyuan Zheng, Yanan Xie, Cheng Chang, Yiheng Shu, Huan Sun, Yu Su · The Ohio State University & Orby AI
venue ICLR 2025 citations 374 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

A GUI agent has, in principle, a very simple body. To operate a computer the way a person does it needs only two things: eyes that see the screen and a hand that can click or type at any pixel. Yet almost every GUI agent in 2024 assumed far more. For perception it read the page's underlying text — the raw HTML or the accessibility (a11y) tree — instead of, or alongside, the screenshot. For action it didn't click a coordinate; it picked an item from a candidate list, either filtered HTML elements or numbered Set-of-Mark boxes overlaid on the image. Building that list, in turn, required the very HTML or a11y tree the agent was supposed to be freed from.

Those crutches are noisy and expensive. Full HTML is bloated with irrelevant markup; the a11y tree is cleaner but depends on developers annotating their apps correctly, and they largely don't — a 2024 survey of the top one million home pages found 95.9% had accessibility-conformance errors, averaging 56.8 errors per page. The cost is just as real: the authors cite earlier work finding HTML can take up to 10× more tokens to encode than the corresponding screenshot, and fetching an a11y tree on desktop or mobile is slow in itself. Every extra token and every extra millisecond compounds over a long-horizon task.

So the authors ask how far a human-like agent — visual observation only, pixel-level action only — can go. The thing standing in the way is grounding: mapping a textual plan ("click the search bar at the top of the page") to a precise on-screen coordinate. A grounding model for agents needs three properties at once: high accuracy (one wrong click can derail the whole task), cross-platform generalization (web, Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS), and flexibility (it should plug into whatever MLLM is doing the planning, not be welded to one model). Prior visual grounding models for GUIs — CogAgent, SeeClick — met none of the three well enough to displace text-based agents. That is the gap UGround targets.

// how it works

The method

The framework is SeeAct-V, a vision-only adaptation of the SeeAct agent. It splits each step into two specialists. A planner — a general MLLM (GPT-4 or GPT-4o in the experiments) — looks at only the screenshot plus the task and emits a textual plan: an element description, an action (Click, Type, Scroll…), and any value. A separate grounding model, UGround, then reads the same screenshot and that description and returns a single pixel coordinate to act on. No HTML, no a11y tree, no candidate list — the planner never has to choose from options, and the executor acts directly on pixels.

UGround is deliberately plain. The backbone is a 7B LLaVA-NeXT model (the paper also reports a UGround-V1 series built on Qwen2-VL). It is asked, every time, "In the screenshot, what are the pixel element coordinates corresponding to {Description}?" and answers with raw numbers — no coordinate normalization — decoded autoregressively like any other text.

UGround : ( screenshot S,  description d )  ⟶  ( x, y ) // the pixel center of the referred element, emitted as plain digits — e.g. "(1344, 1344)" — not a normalized [0,1] box

Two adaptations make a small LLaVA work on full-resolution GUIs. First, resolution: GUI screenshots need ~1,000px+ to stay legible, so the authors widen LLaVA's AnyRes tiling to 36 ViT slices, swap in a CLIP@224px encoder for finer splitting, and push the maximum supported input to 1,344×1,344 (landscape) and 896×2,016 (portrait), with a 16K-context Vicuna to hold all the visual tokens. Second, they drop AnyRes's usual low-resolution global-fusion image — at 224px the global view is too coarse to help on a dense UI.

The real contribution: 10M synthetic grounding examples

The recipe's heart is data. The authors synthesize the largest GUI-grounding dataset to date — 10M elements across 1.3M screenshots — and the bulk of it, Web-Hybrid (9M elements over 773K screenshots), is generated automatically from Common Crawl webpages. Webpages are ideal because they have a dual representation: you get the rendered pixels and the HTML, so element-to-bounding-box correspondences come for free, and HTML attributes (inner-text, alt, aria-label) are a rich source of descriptions.

The key insight is that people refer to UI elements in three distinct ways, and prior grounding data over-indexed on only one. UGround's pipeline deliberately covers all three.

A hybrid synthesis pipeline mixes rules and LLMs. Primary descriptors are harvested from HTML attributes, then enriched by an open MLLM (LLaVA-NeXT-13B) that looks at the element's rendering to add visual/functional cues — producing composite phrases like "hollow heart" or world knowledge like "a blue bird icon represents Twitter" — and tidied by Llama-3-8B-Instruct. Positional and contextual references ("at the top of the page," "the input field labeled Birthday") come from curated rules over each element's coordinates and DOM structure. Web-Hybrid is then complemented by Web-Direct (408K GPT-4o-written, more open-ended REs) and several human-annotated Android sets (GUIAct, AndroidControl, Widget Caption, UIBert, AITZ). Crucially, no desktop data is used in training at all — the bet is that web grounding generalizes.

// what they found

The results

The evaluation is unusually broad: six benchmarks across web, desktop and mobile, in three tiers — pure grounding, offline agents (cached states), and online agents (live environments). The pattern repeats everywhere: UGround beats prior grounding models by a wide margin, and a screenshot-only SeeAct-V agent matches or beats agents that get extra text input.

On ScreenSpot (1,272 single-step grounding instructions), in the standard setting UGround averages 73.3 vs SeeClick's 53.4 and CogAgent's 47.4 — about a 20-point absolute jump — while GPT-4o alone manages just 18.3. Under the harder agent setting (a GPT-4o planner writes the referring expressions), UGround hits 81.4 vs SeeClick's 52.3, a ~29-point gap. The Qwen2-VL-based UGround-V1-7B pushes the standard average to 86.3. Tellingly, UGround is strongest on icons and widgets — the cases that demand semantic and contextual understanding — and does remarkably well on desktop despite never seeing a desktop screenshot in training.

ScreenSpot (standard) — grounding modelAvg. acc.
GPT-4o18.3
CogAgent47.4
SeeClick (prior SOTA)53.4
UGround (LLaVA-7B)73.3
UGround-V1-7B (Qwen2-VL)86.3

The agent tiers tell the same story with end-to-end metrics. Offline: on Multimodal-Mind2Web, SeeAct-V + UGround (GPT-4o) reaches 46.8 element accuracy, beating not only SeeClick (32.9) but the HTML-based Choice (42.3) and SoM (25.6) strategies — even though it never sees the HTML those baselines rely on. On AndroidControl it scores 48.4 / 62.4 (high-/low-level step accuracy) vs the text-only Choice baseline's 42.1 / 55.0; on OmniACT (desktop) it reaches action score 32.8 vs DetACT's 17.0 with image+text. Online: on Mind2Web-Live it gets completion rate 50.7 / success 23.1 (GPT-4) against the text baseline's 44.3 / 21.1; on AndroidWorld, 32.8% success (GPT-4o) beats the SoM variant of M3A (25.4), and UGround-V1-7B lifts that to 44.0%.

Two analyses explain why the data recipe works. The scaling curve shows accuracy climbing with Web-Hybrid size but flattening after ~100K screenshots — and, strikingly, with only 50K screenshots (~600K elements) UGround already beats SeeClick, which was trained on ~3M elements over ~400K screenshots, by more than 10 points. Quality, not just quantity. The ablation confirms Web-Hybrid is load-bearing: trained on Web-Hybrid alone it scores 76.9 (agent setting), versus 73.4 on the supplementary data alone; combined, 81.4 — with the biggest Web-Hybrid gains on icons and widgets.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The central claim — that a vision-only agent matches HTML-based SOTA — is real, but most of the daylight in the headline numbers comes from grounding-isolated metrics, not end-to-end task success. On Multimodal-Mind2Web the authors report element accuracy and explicitly "omit operation scores because they are orthogonal to grounding comparisons." That's a defensible way to isolate the variable, but it means the splashy wins (46.8 vs 42.3) are about picking the right element, not finishing the task. The honest end-to-end ceiling is the online tier, and there the numbers are sober: 23.1% task success on live web (Mind2Web-Live) and 32.8% (GPT-4o) to 44.0% (UGround-V1-7B) on AndroidWorld. UGround removes a wall; it does not make the agents reliable.

The paper's own error analysis is the best evidence here, and it cuts both ways. Across most benchmarks, planning errors — the MLLM describing the wrong element — dominate failures, which is a genuine validation of UGround's grounding. But on ScreenSpot-Desktop grounding errors are still 46.5% of failures and on ScreenSpot-Mobile 27.7%, driven by idiosyncratic long-tail icons (a stylized dollar sign for Zelle, two cartoon people for an Outlook contact list). The "works on desktop without desktop training" story is impressive, but desktop is also where grounding breaks most and OmniACT's absolute action score (32.8) stays low.

A second caution is the base-model confound. The original UGround (LLaVA-7B) scores 73.3 on ScreenSpot; UGround-V1 (Qwen2-VL) scores 86.3 — a 13-point swing from a stronger backbone, not from the recipe. The baselines (SeeClick, CogAgent) ride older, weaker base models trained on far less data (SeeClick ~3M elements vs UGround's 10M), so the "20% absolute" headline bundles together recipe, data scale, and architecture. The authors deserve credit for adding the Qwen2-VL UGround-V1 series as a fairer control — but the marquee comparison isn't a clean ablation of the recipe alone. It doesn't help that most baseline numbers are lifted verbatim from prior papers (Cheng et al., Pan et al., Rawles et al.) rather than re-run under one planner and prompt, and that the Mind2Web SoM baseline is measured on "subsets of 30 tasks for each split."

baselines

SeeClick, CogAgent, Choice/SoM, DetACT — but mostly taken from prior papers, on weaker backbones trained on ≤3M elements. The cleanest control is the in-house Qwen2-VL UGround-V1 series, not the headline SeeClick gap.

contamination

ScreenSpot is small (1,272 single-step items) and now near-saturated (UGround-V1-72B hits 89.4). The agent-setting REs are GPT-4/4o-generated, so that eval mixes planner quality into a "grounding" score; no explicit Common-Crawl-vs-ScreenSpot leakage check.

what's missed

End-to-end success is the real ceiling and it's modest (23.1% web, 32.8% mobile). The 10×-token efficiency that motivates the whole approach is asserted, never measured — no latency or cost numbers for SeeAct-V vs the HTML baselines.

would raise confidence

Re-run every baseline under one planner/prompt; report end-to-end task success and wall-clock/cost as the headline; human-audit the synthetic RE accuracy (LLaVA-13B + Llama-3 generated); add a contamination check and some desktop training data.

// bottom line

A convincing proof that pixel-only perception plus a strong, cheap grounding model can match agents that lean on HTML and accessibility trees — grounding is no longer the bottleneck for GUI agents. But "navigate the digital world as humans do" is carried mostly by grounding-isolated metrics; end-to-end, by the authors' own analysis, the planner is now the limiting factor, live-task success is still ~20–44%, and the efficiency argument that justifies going vision-only is never actually quantified.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Grounding was the wall for vision-only GUI agents, and a 7B model clears it. Trained on 10M synthetic web elements, UGround beats SeeClick by ~20 points (standard) and ~29 points (agent setting) on ScreenSpot, letting a screenshot-only agent match HTML-based SOTA across six benchmarks.
  2. Web-only synthetic data generalizes — and quality beats quantity. UGround is never trained on desktop screenshots yet leads on desktop grounding, and with 50K screenshots it already beats SeeClick's 400K. GUI design regularities transfer across platforms; a good synthesis pipeline is worth more than raw volume.
  3. The bottleneck moved, it didn't vanish. By the authors' own error analysis, planning (the MLLM choosing the right element) now dominates failures, and end-to-end success is still only ~23% on live web and ~44% on mobile. Vision-only embodiment is demonstrated to be feasible — not solved.
